narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and an LSAT score of 35, you've put together a respectable profile for law school, including plans to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 69%, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of students struggling post-graduation. The primary driver behind this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the typical average of around 5.5—and strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar exam challenges. This factor outweighed others, pushing the prediction heavily toward failure despite your other strengths. Your LSAT score, while slightly below the average of 36.7, worked in your favor by nudging the model toward expecting a pass, showing it as a relative bright spot in your application. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, though under the dataset average of 3.21, provided some positive counterbalance, suggesting potential for success that tempered the overall risk slightly. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your Year 3 performance stands out as the biggest deviation, where most successful applicants rank much higher in their deciles. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 90%) of students and didn't sway things much, it fits the norm for those who succeed. This combination left the model convinced that, on balance, your bar exam odds weren't strong enough for admission. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or strengthening your final-year grades to climb those school deciles, as even modest improvements there could flip future predictions. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources or mentorship from alumni who overcame similar early hurdles. With your determination, refining these key areas could open doors at other programs soon.
